judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 1 covers all four expectations solidly, including the RESULTS_DB collision risk with a concrete mitigation (set a private RESULTS_DB path); Report 2 matches on a–c but explicitly dismisses the RESULTS_DB path as irrelevant ("never read... don't need"), missing expectation (d) entirely — so Report 1 answers the task more completely.

report_1.evidence: Batch A bundles three tiny edits to the same 11-line file (`pipeline/registry.py`) rather than splitting them across three engineers — there's no real parallelism to gain from three people each touching one line of a 12-line file, only merge friction to lose.

report_2.evidence: Engineer B — `registry.py`: F-02, F-05, F-09. Three independent one-line constant fixes in the same tiny file — bundle them into one PR so no one has to rebase past someone else's one-line change in an 11-line file.

report_1.evidence: Wave 1 (fully parallel, no shared files, no open decisions): Batches A, B, C, D run concurrently, each in its own worktree/branch off one shared integration branch tip. ... Batch B pairs F-01 ... with F-11 ...

report_2.evidence: Wave 1 (start immediately, in parallel): Engineers A, B, C, D start on their fixes. ... Engineer C — rubric.py: F-01 first, then F-11 (new edge-case test) written against the fixed parse().

report_1.evidence: picking new numbers is a user-facing scoring-policy decision, not a bug fix — it changes the meaning of every score the pipeline has ever produced or will produce. Per project documentation ("flag anything that would change user-facing behavior and isn't already a settled decision... don't guess"), I'd escalate this to whoever owns grading policy for concrete replacement numbers before any engineer touches the code

report_2.evidence: F-06 is a policy decision dressed as a bug fix. ... Changing these numbers changes scoring output for every existing/future evaluation run, which affects comparability of historical scores. That's a call for whoever owns the grading quality bar (reviewer/product), not something an engineer should pick unilaterally. I'd get explicit target numbers (or an explicit tolerance) before assigning.

report_1.evidence: `tests/conftest.py` hardcodes `RESULTS_DB = os.environ.get("RESULTS_DB", "/var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db")` — a shared filesystem path outside the repo. ... two engineers' local test runs on the same machine, or two parallel CI jobs, would collide on that path. Each engineer should set `RESULTS_DB` to a private scratch path defensively.

report_2.evidence: RESULTS_DB in tests/conftest.py is defined but never read. Engineers don't need /var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db or any real model endpoint to exist to do this work
